posts.cooked:
• 2019 Daniel Bouland Morgon Les Délys Vignes plantee en 1926 - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Morgon (6/27/2021)
    This is lovely, and the fruit is on the darker side of the spectrum. Scents of blackberries, blueberries, tomato leaf, and earth. The palate is medium bodied, with a sense of warmth on the palate that reminds me of a sun-ripened plum, fruity, juicy, & warm. It’s making me wonder if 2019 is in some ways going to be similar to 2009.

768×1024 102 KB

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Sounds like 2018 which was too OTT for my palate! I generally love this stuff, however. I think that I have only had Thivin in 2019 thus far. What’s your overall sense of the vintage?

Sounds like 2018 which was too OTT for my palate! I generally love this stuff, however. I think that I have only had Thivin in 2019 thus far. What’s your overall sense of the vintage?

My go-to’s tend to be in the more red-fruited range. Coudert is my #1 as far as quantity, followed by Thivin in the cellar. Metras is a long-standing favorite, but getting harder & harder to get in any quantity. For me, this this is “very Morgon”, if that makes any sense. I think the age of the vines is likely to impart a level of intensity in the fruit. While I reference 2009, there is still a lovely balance in this wine that I appreciate and enjoy. I have two more bottles, and I will enjoy checking in on them over time, but this is a wine I will not be seeking more of. Right now, it’s gots space in the Eurocave, but not for long. I plan to move the remaining bottles to the in-laws basement. This is a wine I think they’ll really like.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
I am not of the opinion that 2019 is in any way overripe, either at Bouland or more generally in Beaujolais from what i’ve tasted so far.

I’ve had the 2019 Bouland Morgon Bellevue ‘Cailloux’ twice this year, and was mighty impressed on both occasions: really precise, red fruited and fresh, a style I like in Beaujolais. Perhaps less depth than Thivin, but brighter and stunning value. I’m puzzled why I only discovered Bouland a little over 1 year ago! Anyway, playing catch-up now!

Here my notes, for what it’s worth:

7 Jan 2021: Wow, very impressive indeed! Deep garnet colour. Nose is slightly reserved (bottle was quite cold though) but showing some vivid red fruit, raspberry, flowers, certain creaminess. Palate is a delight, with a striking purity of red fruit, very focused. Lifted by laser beam acidity and buttressed by a rocky finish. Excellent stuff.

26 Jun 2021: Bouland strikes again! Rather than a wine of layers, to me this evokes a sense of facets on a gemstone, showing a different nature as you turn it through the light… red currant, pomegranate, violet, wet stone, cinnamon… great freshness and detail on the palate, light bodied and crunchy, this showed terrific once again. Got to be one of the top QPRs on planet wine!

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
My wife and I just created a tasting for ourselves with known wines in which we double blinded the '19 Thivin CdB, '19 Thivin Reverdon Brouilly, '19 Domaine du Pavillon de Chavannes CdB, and '18 Bouland Morgon Corcelette Foudre N. 3. My recall isn’t perfect, but I’m pretty sure we both whiffed on guessing the Morgon and liked all of the wines (we had only not had the Reverdon and the Pavillon de Chavannes). I think my rank was Chavannes > Thivin CdB > Thivin Reverdon > Bouland. I only guessed the Thivin CdB correctly. My wife’s rank was Thivin CdB > Thivin Reverdon > Chavannes > Bouland. My impression was that the Chavannes was slightly more well-rounded, but we were eating while tasting in a very unscientific way and really had a tough time setting the final order.

I’ve only recently been introduced to Bouland and Thivin, so I don’t have the frame of reference with back vintages or aged product to understand where these are headed, but I’m excited to see. They are great to drink right now, though, and we had fun which is most important. When I get my hands on a '19 Bouland of some sort, I’m excited to compare it to the '18 and the '18 les Delys Cuve N. 9 that I have also.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
What’s with the Bouland cuvées being labeled by Foudre number? Is this specific to the US market? I have not seen these cuvées or labelings in France, and write ups of visits to the Domaine on the French language wine board don’t seem to mention these. Instead, the Corcellete, Belleville (and maybe Delys?) cuvées are labeled as ‘Sable’ or ‘Cailloux’…

Anyone have any insight here?

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked: Just had the 2019 Bouland Delys the other night. Started with a touch of reduction, but with about one half hour of air showed a powerful, surprisingly dark profile for a Beaujolais with exceptional balance and penetration. Very good, and bound to get even better with some bottle of age. I bought another yesterday, spotting some more at a certain retailer who specializes in $25 flat shipping to California…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
What’s with the Bouland cuvées being labeled by Foudre number? Is this specific to the US market? I have not seen these cuvées or labelings in France, and write ups of visits to the Domaine on the French language wine board don’t seem to mention these. Instead, the Corcellete, Belleville (and maybe Delys?) cuvées are labeled as ‘Sable’ or ‘Cailloux’…

Anyone have any insight here?

This was all explained in TWA, if you happen to subscribe!

After experimenting with various ways of designating his various terroirs—including the use of foudre numbers—Bouland has now [from the 2018 vintage onwards] alighted upon a much clearer solution: he simply distinguishes between “Cailloux” (stones) and “Sable” (sand) on the label, as well as differentiating between “Vieilles Vignes” and regular bottlings. Readers should take a moment to ensure that the bottling they are purchasing corresponds in both particulars with the accompanying reviews.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked: Thanks William, much appreciated!

posts.name: Charlie Gierling
posts.cooked: Could not find a lieu dit called Les Delys in Villie Morgon but a place called Les Delys. The place is part of the lieu dit Corcelette (Weinlagen). So the question is: what is Les Delys? Probaly a vineyard near that place. Does anyone have better information? I would like to add the vineyard to weinlagen-info

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Could not find a lieu dit called Les Delys in Villie Morgon but a place called Les Delys. The place is part of the lieu dit Corcelette (Weinlagen). So the question is: what is Les Delys? Probaly a vineyard near that place. Does anyone have better information? I would like to add the vineyard to weinlagen-info

Delys is a sous-climat of Corcelette, I think, in the way that Butteaux is a sous-climat of Montamains in Chablis for example.

As for Bouland’s parcel, it’s right behind his new winery and home (which are still under construction in the photo on google maps) here: Google Maps

Daniel didn’t turn up for our last tasting together at his little tasting room in the village, so since I had a bit of time in the program, I drove around his various parcels and found him in the middle of Les Delys, applying a treatment with an atomizer, and dragged him out so I could review the 2020s.

And here’s looking downhill: Google Maps

posts.name: Charlie Gierling
posts.cooked: Thank you. The position is clear, but probably it is bigger than this Weinlagen

posts.name: Charlie Gierling
posts.cooked: Is “sous-climat” the term they use in Burgundy for such a plot?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Is “sous-climat” the term they use in Burgundy for such a plot?

Yes, in Burgundy. Not sure if they have a different term for it in Beaujolais.

In any case, my thinking was that Delys is to Corcellette what Javernières is to Côte du Py…

Looking at the geological maps produced for the Inter Beaujolais, which feature the lieux-dits, Delys doesn’t feature on the map however: Morgon - Beaujolais

But that could just be because it’s an old local usage that hasn’t been formally recognized…

posts.cooked:

Anyone have any insight here?

This was all explained in TWA, if you happen to subscribe!

After experimenting with various ways of designating his various terroirs—including the use of foudre numbers—Bouland has now [from the 2018 vintage onwards] alighted upon a much clearer solution: he simply distinguishes between “Cailloux” (stones) and “Sable” (sand) on the label, as well as differentiating between “Vieilles Vignes” and regular bottlings. Readers should take a moment to ensure that the bottling they are purchasing corresponds in both particulars with the accompanying reviews.

Hold on young fella, why does my 2019 mention Foudre 8?

Was wondering if someone would ask this! For Delys, there’s a foudre (number 8) and a tank, or “cuve” (number 9). But, it’s the same parcel, picked the same day, and the same vinification. And while the élevage is a bit different, the wines are essentially the same. So the differences are much smaller than those previous designated by Bouland’s system of numbering foudres (which, when he applied to other cuvées, I don’t think he ever did for Delys btw).
